latest.title: Full Year Results 2023
latest.content:
22 Sep 2023 07:00
RNS Number : 2957N
Mothercare PLC
22 September 2023
Mothercare plc
Full Year Results 2023
Further progress made on the journey towards becoming an asset-light, global franchising business
Mothercare plc ("Mothercare", "the Company" or "the Group"), the
highly trusted British heritage brand, that connects with the parents of newborn babies and children across multiple product categories throughout their early life as parents,, today announces full year results for the 52 week period to 25 March 2023. Comparatives are based on the 52 week period to 26 March 2022.
Key Highlights
•
9% increase in net worldwide retail sales by franchise partners in continuing markets of £322.7 million (2022: £297.1 million excluding Russia, £385.3 million including Russia).
•
Adjusted EBITDA of £6.7 million (2022: £12.0 million), ahead of analysts' expectations.
•
Net borrowings of £12.4 million (2022: £9.9 million) at the year end.
•
Pension scheme deficit materially reduced to £35.0 million (March 2020: £124.6 million).
•
Pension contributions reduced by £38.8 million to £34.9 million.
Current Trading & Outlook
·
In the first twenty five weeks of FY24, the Group's Franchise Partners recorded total retail sales of £132.5 million (FY23: £156.8 million), with the decline largely resulting from the continuing challenges in our Middle Eastern markets
·
We expect to complete a refinancing shortly and remain in discussions with a number of key stakeholders and financing partners, to ensure that the Group has adequate and appropriate financing for the future.
·
Our medium-term guidance for the steady state operation, in more normal circumstances, of our continuing franchise operations remains that they are capable of exceeding £10 million operating profit and that opportunities exist to grow our global footprint further.
·
We are now focused on both restoring critical mass and monetising the Mothercare global brand IP.
Financial Highlights
·
Loss for the 52 weeks to 25 March 2023 of £0.1 million (2022: £12.1 million profit).
·
Net debt3 at £12.9 million (2022: £11.0 million).

Clive Whiley, Chairman of Mothercare, commented:
"I am pleased with the progress Mothercare has made during the year as we continue our transformation towards an asset-light, global franchising business. Our priority over the last 12 months has been the continued execution of our Transformation Plan and cementing Mothercare's future as a sustainable business model, for the benefit of all our stakeholders.
I would like to thank our colleagues across the business, alongside our pension trustees and all other stakeholders, for their continuing support. Without this, we would not be in the profitable, cash generative position we are today.
We have a compelling market opportunity. Mothercare remains in an unparalleled position of being a highly trusted British heritage brand, with a significant opportunity to leverage this brand equity and grow our global presence beyond our existing franchise network. There is still work to do, but we are excited about the future prospects for Mothercare as we leave behind the turmoil of recent years."

Mothercare plc

Notes
The Directors believe that alternative performance measures ("APMs") assist in providing additional useful information on the performance and position of the Group and across the period because it is consistent with how business performance is reported to the Board and Operating Board.
APMs are also used to enhance the comparability of information between reporting periods and geographical units (such as like-for-like sales), by adjusting for non-recurring or uncontrollable factors which affect IFRS measures, to aid the user in understanding the Groups performance. Consequently, APMs are used by the Directors and management for performance analysis, planning, reporting and incentive setting purposes. The key APMs that the Group has focused on in the period are as set out in the Glossary.
1 - Worldwide retail

latest.title: Annual Report and Accounts and Notice of AGM
latest.content:
25 Sep 2023 07:00
Annual Report and Accounts and Notice of AGM
25 September 2023
Mothercare plc ("the Company")
NOTICE OF AGM AND POSTING OF ANNUAL REPORT
Mothercare plc, the leading specialist global brand for parents and young children confirms that, on 25 September 2023, it posted its Annual Report and Accounts for the year ended 25 March 2023 together with its Notice of Annual General Meeting (“AGM”). The AGM will be held at 11.00am on 23 October 2023 at Westside 1, London Road, Hemel Hempstead, HP3 9TD.
Shareholders are encouraged to submit their proxy votes ahead of the meeting and appoint the chair of the AGM as their proxy. Shareholders are also invited to send questions relating to the business of the meeting by email to: investorrelations@mothercare.com to be received by 11.00 am on 19 October 2023.
Copies of both the Annual Report and Accounts and the AGM Notice are available from the Company’s website at www.mothercareplc.com/investors.
Lynne MediniGroup Company SecretaryMothercare plc
Investor and analyst enquiries to:Mothercare plc Email: investorrelations@mothercare.comClive Whiley, ChairmanAndrew Cook, Chief Financial Officer
Numis Securities Limited Tel: 020 7260 1000(NOMAD & Joint Corporate Broker) Luke BordewichHenry Slater
Cavendish Capital Markets Tel: 020 7220 0500(Joint Corporate Broker) Carl Holmes
Media enquiries to: MHP Email: mothercare@mhpgroup.comRachel Farrington Tel: 020 3128 8613Tim Rowntree

company_30s.what_they_do: MTC.L is a retailer that sells a mix of home‑goods and apparel through both physical stores and an online platform. Its core business is to provide a curated shopping experience that blends traditional retail with e‑commerce convenience.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The company sits in a sector that is still struggling to balance brick‑and‑mortar revenue with the growth of online sales. Its ability to adapt to consumer shifts and to secure new licensing deals can set it apart from competitors that are stuck in legacy models.
company_30s.current_state: MTC.L has been in a sharp decline, with cash burn from pension obligations and debt repayment putting pressure on operations. The shares are trading well below the price they reached a year ago, indicating a steep valuation drop. The company is now focusing on refinancing and restructuring, hoping to stabilize cash flow while pursuing new partnerships.

rns_narrative: The latest filings show the company is pushing through a refinancing package and deferring pension contributions to preserve liquidity. A new licensing agreement with a Turkish retailer is also on the books, signalling a push into international markets. Meanwhile, the board is preparing for an AGM where shareholders will vote on the restructuring plan.

These moves suggest a two‑fold strategy: first, shore up the balance sheet; second, create new revenue streams through overseas licensing. If the refinancing closes and the license goes live, the company could regain some of its former scale.

The narrative is one of survival and cautious optimism: the company is making concrete steps to address its debt and pension shortfall, but the next few months will decide whether those actions are enough.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Pension crisis fuels wariness, but whispers of a fresh license offer hint at potential revival.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: Some traders see the refinancing and the new licensing deal as a turning point that could unlock value once cash flow stabilises.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Others point to the £6 m pension deficit and heavy debt repayment schedule, arguing the company is on a path to insolvency if the restructuring fails.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The chatter has moved from pure pessimism to a split view: investors are still skeptical, but a few voices are tracking the refinancing deadline as a possible catalyst.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends is flat – no recent search activity, which means the market is still unaware of any upcoming news.

near_term_catalysts: The completion of the refinancing package in the next 4–6 weeks could inject fresh capital and reduce interest pressure.
If the licensing agreement with Ebebek Mağazacılık A.Ş. is activated, the company could start generating additional revenue streams in a new market.
The AGM scheduled for early July will decide the fate of the restructuring plan; a vote in favour could signal confidence from institutional holders.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a sudden spike in trading volume around the refinancing announcement or the AGM, a break above the 200‑day moving average, and any signs of institutional accumulation on the balance sheet.
expect_next.timing_regime: The shares appear to be in the early stages of a mean‑reversion play – the price has already fallen deeply, and the compression score suggests a tight range. Confirmation will come when the company either secures the refinancing or shows signs of institutional buying.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: If the company completes its refinancing and the license deal goes live, the stock could recover to roughly 50% of its former peak – a 100% upside from the current level. Historically, deep‑crash setups that finish below 70% of their ATH have produced double‑digit returns once a catalyst arrives.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: Failure to secure the refinancing, a rejection at the AGM, or a continued pension shortfall could push the shares to the bottom of the range. The worst‑case scenario is a further decline to a level where the company is effectively worthless, with a downside that could exceed the current price.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential upside of ~2× the entry price against a downside limited to the current market price – a 2:1 reward‑to‑risk ratio that aligns with our contrarian playbook.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter when the share price breaks above the 200‑day moving average with volume at least 1.5× the 20‑day average. Size the position so that a 10% drop would not breach your stop, and set the stop just below the recent low to protect against a brief retracement.
playbook.if_watching: Hold cash until you see a confirmed volume spike on the refinancing announcement or the AGM vote. Watch for institutional accumulation on the balance sheet; a 5% rise in holdings by a major fund could be a green light.
playbook.if_passing: If the company fails to close the refinancing or the AGM votes against the restructuring, consider cutting the position, as the fundamental problems remain unaddressed.

ai_insights.bottom_line: MTC.L sits on a deep crash floor, but a refinancing deadline and a new international license offer create a clear catalyst window; if these play out, the upside is at least double the entry price, making it a high‑asymmetry contrarian bet.
